does: This paper conducts a systematic investigation into how preference data characteristics affect Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a method for aligning language models with human preferences without requiring a separate reward model. Through theoretical analysis and extensive experiments, the authors demonstrate that the quality of chosen (preferred) responses is the dominant factor driving DPO performance, while the quality of rejected responses has relatively limited impact. They characterize the optimal response distribution under the DPO objective mathematically and show that contrastiveness between response pairs primarily helps by improving chosen samples. The work also analyzes online DPO, revealing that it effectively reduces to supervised fine-tuning on chosen responses, supported by experiments across diverse tasks showing consistent performance improvements from enhancing chosen response quality.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on preference-based alignment for large language models, building directly on DPO which itself emerged as a simpler alternative to reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) and reward model-based approaches. It responds to the open question of what makes preference data effective by connecting to broader research on data quality in machine learning, preference learning theory, and understanding empirically what matters in training large language models. The paper relates to concurrent work examining data efficiency and quality in language model alignment, as well as theoretical work on preference optimization objectives. It also engages with the supervised fine-tuning literature, showing connections between different alignment paradigms.
enables: These findings provide a foundation for improving DPO in practice by clarifying where data collection and curation efforts should focus—specifically on generating high-quality chosen responses rather than equally emphasizing both sides of preference pairs. The theoretical characterization of optimal response distributions could inform future preference dataset construction methods and synthetic data generation strategies. The insight that online DPO reduces to fine-tuning on chosen responses suggests new directions for hybrid alignment approaches combining preference learning with standard supervised learning. This work may enable more efficient alignment procedures by guiding resource allocation in preference data creation, and opens avenues for studying how to automatically improve chosen response quality or select which pairs to include in training.
